1: Psalm 60 is a national lament after military defeat — the Israelite communal parallel to Psalm 44's innocent-suffering. The superscription gives a precise historical setting: David's war with Aram-naharaim and Aram-zobah, while Joab struck down 12,000 Edomites in the Valley of Salt (cf. 2 Sam 8:13; 1 Chr 18:12 — where 18,000 are listed; the variance may reflect different phases of the campaign or different counting). The tune: "Lily of the Testimony" (shûshan ʿêdût). אֱלֹהִים זְנַחְתָּנוּ פְרַצְתָּנוּ (ʾĕlōhîm zĕnaḥtānû pĕraṣtānû, "O God, you have rejected us and broken our defenses") — zānaḥ: the covenant-rejection word (as in Ps 44:9, 23; 43:2). Pāraṣ: to break through, to burst through (as a wall is breached). אָנַפְתָּ תְשׁוֹבֵב לָנוּ (ʾānaphtā tĕshôbêb lānû, "you have been angry — restore us!") — ʾānaph: to snort in anger (the heavy, angry breathing); shûb (polel: to restore, to bring back).
2: הִרְעַשְׁתָּה אֶרֶץ פְּצַמְתָּהּ (hirʿashtāh ʾereṣ pĕṣamtāhh, "You have made the earth shake; you have cracked it open") — rāʿash (hiphil: to cause to shake — the earthquake verb of Ps 18:7); pāṣam: to split, to crack (rare — the fissure of a broken landscape). The military defeat is described in geological imagery: the land itself fractured. רְפָה שְׁבָרֶיהָ כִי-מָטָה (rĕpāh shĕbārêhā kî-māṭāh, "heal its fractures, for it is collapsing") — rāpāʾ: to heal (as in Ps 41:4); māṭāh: to totter (the instability verb of Ps 46:2, 5, 6). The earthquake of defeat needs divine healing.
3: הִרְאִיתָה עַמְּךָ קָשָׁה (hirʾîtāh ʿammĕkā qāshāh, "You have put your people through severe trials") — qāsheh: hard, severe (the hardships that prove and test). הִשְׁקִיתָנוּ יַיִן תַּרְעֵלָה (hishqîtānû yayin tarʿêlāh, "you have made us drink disorienting wine") — yayin: wine; tarʿêlāh: a staggering, a reeling (from rāʿal: to quake, to tremble — so "wine of reeling/staggering"). God the host serving disorienting wine to his own people — the military confusion of defeat experienced as divine intoxication. The image appears also in Isa 51:17-22: the "cup of staggering" that Jerusalem must drink.
4: נָתַתָּה לִירֵאֶיךָ נֵס (nātatā lîrêʾêkā nês, "You have set up a banner for those who fear you") — nês: a standard, a flag-pole, a rallying banner (the military signal for gathering troops — Num 21:8-9 [Moses' bronze serpent on a nês]; Isa 11:12 [the banner for the nations]). לְהִתְנוֹסֵס מִפְּנֵי קֹשֶׁט סֶלָה (lĕhitnôsês mippĕnê qōshet selāh, "for them to rally to in the face of the enemy's bow") — nāsas (hithpoel: to be lifted up, to display oneself as a banner): the rallying of those who fear God around the divine standard. Even in defeat, there is a banner to gather around.
5: לְמַעַן יֵחָלְצוּן יְדִידֶיךָ (lĕmaʿan yêḥālĕṣûn yĕdîdêkā, "Save your dear ones — rescue them") — yĕdîdîm: the beloved ones, the darlings (the same root as yĕdîdôt of Ps 45 — "Song of Beloved-Ones"): the people as God's beloved. ḥālaṣ (niphal: to be rescued, to be delivered). הוֹשִׁיעָה יְמִינְךָ וַעֲנֵנִי (hôshîʿāh yĕmînĕkā waʿănênî, "rescue them by your right hand, and answer our prayer") — yāmîn: the right hand (the hand of power and of honor — the hand that holds the sword and that blesses). The divine right hand as the instrument of salvation.
6: אֱלֹהִים דִּבֶּר בְּקָדְשׁוֹ (ʾĕlōhîm dibbêr bĕqodsho, "God has spoken in his holiness") — the oracle begins with the highest possible authority marker: divine speech from the divine holiness itself (qōdesh: the sacred, the set-apart realm). אֶעְלֹזָה אֲחַלְּקָה שְׁכֶם (ʾeʿlōzāh ʾăḥallĕqāh shĕkem, "I will triumph; I will divide up Shechem") — ʿālaz: to exult, to triumph; ḥālaq (piel: to divide up, to allocate): the divine warrior dividing the conquered territory. Shechem (the central highland city of Ephraim, Jacob's territory — Gen 33:18-19) is the first claimed. וְעֵמֶק סֻכּוֹת אֲמַדֵּד (wĕʿêmeq sukkôt ʾămaddêd, "and portion out the Valley of Succoth") — Succoth (east of the Jordan, Jacob's camp site in Gen 33:17) measured out — the divine surveyor staking the land.
7: לִי גִלְעָד וְלִי מְנַשֶּׁה (lî gilʿād wĕlî mĕnashsheh, "Gilead is mine, Manasseh is mine") — the Transjordanian territory (Gilead) and the half-tribe of Manasseh claimed by the divine warrior. וְאֶפְרַיִם מָעוֹז רֹאשִׁי (wĕʾeprayim māʿôz rōʾshî, "Ephraim is my helmet") — māʿôz rōʾshî: "stronghold of my head" — the military-helmet image: Ephraim, the largest and strongest northern tribe, is the divine warrior's war-helmet. יְהוּדָה מְחֹקְקִי (yĕhûdāh mĕḥōqĕqî, "Judah is my scepter") — mĕḥōqēq: the one who issues decrees, the ruler's scepter/staff (from ḥāqaq: to inscribe, to decree). Judah as the ruling tribe — echoing Gen 49:10: "the scepter shall not depart from Judah."
8: מוֹאָב סִיר רַחְצִי (mōʾāb sîr raḥṣî, "Moab is my washbasin") — the triumphant humiliation: Moab (Israel's trans-Jordan neighbor, perennial enemy) reduced to the washbasin — the vessel used for the most menial cleansing (the basin in which the warrior washes his feet after battle). עַל-אֱדוֹם אַשְׁלִיךְ נַעֲלִי (ʿal-ʾĕdôm ʾashlîk naʿălî, "over Edom I throw my sandal") — the sandal-throw as a legal sign of ownership and appropriation (as in Ruth 4:7-8 — the kinsman-redeemer's sandal transfer; and Deut 25:9 — the rejected brother's sandal removed). Throwing the sandal over Edom: claiming ownership, treating Edom as a footstool. עָלַי פְּלֶשֶׁת הִתְרוֹעָעִי (ʿālay pĕleshet hitrôʿāʿî, "over Philistia I shout in triumph") — rûaʿ (hithpael: to raise the victory cry against, to shout in triumph over): the Philistines receive not service (as Moab and Edom) but the shout of conquest.
9: מִי יֹבִלֵנִי עִיר מָצוֹר (mî yōbîlênî ʿîr māṣôr, "Who will bring me into the fortified city?") — the oracle of divine ownership (vv6-8) is followed by the community's question: how is this divine claim to be actualized? ʿÎr māṣôr: the besieged/fortified city (the Edomite stronghold of Petra — the rock-city?). מִי נָחַנִי עַד-אֱדוֹם (mî nāḥanî ʿad-ʾĕdôm, "who will guide me all the way to Edom?") — nāḥāh: to lead, to guide (the shepherd-guide verb of Ps 31:3; 43:3). The way to the conquest requires a divine guide.
10: הֲלֹא-אַתָּה אֱלֹהִים זְנַחְתָּנוּ (hălōʾ-ʾattāh ʾĕlōhîm zĕnaḥtānû, "Have you not rejected us, O God?") — the question that restates the opening (v1: zānaḥ): has the God who owns all the territory abandoned his people? The oracle of vv6-8 shows God's territorial claim; the military defeat (v1-3) shows the current reality. וְלֹא-תֵצֵא אֱלֹהִים בְּצִבְאֹתֵינוּ (wĕlōʾ-têṣêʾ ʾĕlōhîm bĕṣibʾôtênû, "You no longer go out, God, with our armies") — the divine warrior who should march at the head of the armies (as in Num 10:35: "Rise up, LORD! May your enemies be scattered!") is absent. The military defeat is the absence of the divine warrior.
11: הָבָה-לָּנוּ עֶזְרָת מִצָּר (hābāh-llānû ʿezrat miṣṣār, "Give us help against the enemy") — hābāh: give (an urgent imperative); ʿezrāh: help (the ʿezer of Gen 2:18 — the helper who is needed). וְשָׁוְא תְּשׁוּעַת אָדָם (wĕshāwĕ tĕshûʿat ʾādām, "for human deliverance is worthless") — shāwĕ: vanity, emptiness (the shāwĕ of the Decalogue: "you shall not take the name of the LORD your God in vain/emptiness" — the same emptiness applied to human military capacity). Tĕshûʿat ʾādām: "the salvation of humanity/man" — human military power is as empty as the misuse of the divine name. The psalm's entire theological argument: the territory belongs to God (vv6-8), but only God can actualize the claim (vv9-12).
12: בֵּאלֹהִים נַעֲשֶׂה-חָיִל (bêʾlōhîm naʿăśeh-ḥāyil, "Through God we will do great deeds") — ḥayil: strength, valor, wealth (the ḥayil of "women of valor" in Prov 31:10 — the same word). The great deeds (ḥāyil) are possible only "through God" (bêʾlōhîm): the prepositional phrase carries the theological weight. וְהוּא יָבוּס צָרֵינוּ (wĕhûʾ yābûs ṣārênû, "he himself will crush our enemies") — bûs: to trample underfoot (as the nations were trampled in Ps 44:5; 47:3). The pronoun hûʾ (he himself) is emphatic: not we, not our armies, but God himself will do the treading-down. The psalm of defeat ends on the note of confident divine victory.
